depthLevels.label: Intro
depthLevels.description: Understand the core idea and vocabulary.
depthLevels.expectedOutcome: Can explain the concept in plain language.

depthLevels.label: Guided Practice
depthLevels.description: Apply the concept with examples and hints.
depthLevels.expectedOutcome: Can solve similar problems with light guidance.

depthLevels.label: Applied
depthLevels.description: Use the concept in realistic scenarios.
depthLevels.expectedOutcome: Can use the concept in project-like situations.

depthLevels.label: Mastery
depthLevels.description: Connect this concept to architecture and trade-offs.
depthLevels.expectedOutcome: Can teach and adapt the concept to new contexts.

concepts.title: Service Facade Pattern
concepts.summary: Encapsulate data access and orchestration behind a small API for UI layers.

relatedTopics.note: Signals provide ergonomic local state in facades.
Facades become more useful when HTTP concerns are standardized.

examples.title: Restaurant analogy
examples.prompt: Why should a component call a facade instead of HttpClient directly?
examples.solution: The component is the diner, facade is the waiter, and HttpClient is the kitchen. The diner should not walk into the kitchen.

concepts.title: Signals for Local Reactive State
concepts.summary: Use Angular signals to keep local state predictable, composable, and side-effect aware.

relatedTopics.note: Facade state can be expressed as signals.

examples.title: Filter by depth
examples.prompt: Implement a computed signal that returns concepts available at a selected depth level.
examples.solution: Use selectedDepth signal and concepts signal with computed(() => concepts().filter(...)).

concepts.title: Dependency Injection and Token Design
concepts.summary: Use DI tokens and provider scopes to swap implementations and isolate side effects.

relatedTopics.note: Facades depend on DI for transport and repositories.

examples.title: Swap data source
examples.prompt: How can DI help replace mock JSON with a real backend later?
examples.solution: Depend on an abstract repository token and provide a different implementation by environment.

concepts.title: Smart/Dumb Components with @Input/@Output
concepts.summary: Smart components own data and logic; dumb components receive data via @Input and emit events via @Output. This hard boundary maximizes reusability and testability.

relatedTopics.note: Dumb components should always use OnPush.

examples.title: Manager and Worker
examples.prompt: Why separate smart from dumb components?
examples.solution: The manager (smart) decides WHAT work to do and WHO does it. Workers (dumb) only know HOW to do their specific task. A worker never calls a service directly — the manager handles all coordination.

examples.title: Typed Input/Output contract
examples.prompt: Create a UserCard dumb component with typed inputs and outputs.

examples.title: Refactor mixed component
examples.prompt: Take a component that fetches users AND renders a list. Split it into a smart UserListContainer and a dumb UserListView.
examples.solution: UserListContainer injects UserService and passes users to child. UserListView has @Input users and @Output selectUser — zero service imports.

examples.title: Reusability across features
examples.prompt: Your ProductCard component injects CartService. Now you need ProductCard in a comparison page without a cart. What went wrong?
examples.solution: ProductCard should be dumb. It emits (addToCart). In the cart page, parent dispatches to CartService. In comparison page, parent ignores the event or shows a different action.

concepts.title: HTTP + Loading State Pattern
concepts.summary: Model async operations as a state machine (idle, loading, success, error) to prevent ambiguous UI states.

examples.title: Traffic light analogy
examples.prompt: Why model loading as a state machine?
examples.solution: A traffic light is always red, yellow, or green — never two at once. LoadingState works the same. Separate booleans (isLoading + hasError) can be true simultaneously, causing UI bugs.

examples.title: LoadingState<T> type
examples.prompt: Define a type-safe loading state.
examples.solution: type LoadingState<T> = { status: 'idle' } | { status: 'loading' } | { status: 'success'; data: T } | { status: 'error'; error: string };

examples.title: Implement full loading flow
examples.prompt: Create a PostsFacade that exposes a LoadingState<Post[]> signal and handles all states.
examples.solution: loadPosts() sets state to loading, calls HTTP, maps to success/error. Template uses @switch on status. Error state shows a retry button.

examples.title: Boolean hell anti-pattern
examples.prompt: isLoading is true, hasError is false, data is null. After a failed request, isLoading is still true. What happens?
examples.solution: The UI shows a spinner forever. With LoadingState<T>, this is impossible: setting status to 'error' automatically removes the 'loading' state.

concepts.title: NgRx Store Pattern
concepts.summary: Centralize shared state with the Redux pattern: Actions describe events, Reducers compute state, Selectors derive views.

relatedTopics.note: Use signals for local state, NgRx for shared/complex state.

examples.title: Bank ledger analogy
examples.prompt: How does NgRx manage state?
examples.solution: A bank ledger (Store) records every transaction (Action). The accountant (Reducer) computes the new balance. The statement (Selector) shows the data. You never modify the ledger directly.

examples.title: Counter feature with createFeature
examples.prompt: Define a minimal NgRx feature with actions, reducer, and selectors.
examples.solution: export const counterFeature = createFeature({ name: 'counter', reducer: createReducer(0, on(increment, state => state + 1)) });

examples.title: Add a priority feature
examples.prompt: Add a 'priority' property to todos with actions, reducer updates, and a selector.
examples.solution: Define setPriority action. Update reducer with on(setPriority). Create selector: createSelector(selectTodos, todos => todos.filter(t => t.priority === 'high')).

examples.title: Store vs Signal decision
examples.prompt: Your app has a theme toggle (dark/light). Store or signal?
examples.solution: Signal. Theme is simple and binary. NgRx adds boilerplate for no benefit. Rule: if state is shared across routes AND complex, use NgRx. Otherwise, signals.

concepts.title: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush
concepts.summary: Optimize performance by telling Angular to skip re-rendering unless @Input references change, events fire, or signals emit.

examples.title: Doorbell analogy
examples.prompt: What is the difference between Default and OnPush?
examples.solution: Default: Angular checks every room after any noise. OnPush: Angular only checks a room when the doorbell rings (input changed) or someone inside calls out (event/signal).

examples.title: OnPush + Signals
examples.prompt: Set up an OnPush component that reacts to signal changes.
examples.solution:
@Component({ changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush, template: `

{{ count() }}

` }) export class Counter { count = signal(0); }

examples.title: Find the mutation bug
examples.prompt: A parent does items.push(newItem). The OnPush child does not update. Fix it.
examples.solution: The parent must create a new array reference: this.items = [...this.items, newItem]. OnPush only detects reference changes.

examples.title: Performance profiling
examples.prompt: A dashboard with 50 cards re-renders all cards on every click. Fix it.
examples.solution: Enable OnPush on all cards. Use trackBy in @for loops. Result: only the clicked card re-renders, vastly improving performance.

concepts.title: RxJS Operators: switchMap, combineLatest, takeUntilDestroyed
concepts.summary: Master the operators that solve common Angular async patterns: search cancellation, stream combination, and automatic cleanup.

examples.title: TV channel analogy
examples.prompt: What does switchMap do?
examples.solution: switchMap is like changing TV channels. You stop watching channel 3 immediately when you switch to 5. Similarly, switchMap cancels the previous HTTP request when a new search term arrives.

examples.title: Search with switchMap
examples.prompt: Implement a typeahead search that cancels stale requests.
examples.solution: this.searchCtrl.valueChanges.pipe(debounceTime(300), switchMap(q => this.http.get(`/api/search?q=${q}`)));

examples.title: Combine filters
examples.prompt: Combine category$ and sort$ filters with an HTTP stream.
examples.solution: combineLatest([category$, sort$]).pipe(switchMap(([cat, sort]) => http.get(`/api/items?c=${cat}&s=${sort}`)));

examples.title: Memory leak diagnosis
examples.prompt: A component subscribes to a WebSocket but never unsubscribes, causing leaks on navigation.
examples.solution: Add takeUntilDestroyed(this.destroyRef) to the pipe. It automatically cleans up the subscription when the component is destroyed.
